1: Praise the LORD! Praise the name of the LORD; praise him, all you servants. The name of the LORD that is worthy of endless praise is the name Jesus, the LORD who saves (Matthew 1:21); at his name every knee will bow (Philippians 2:9-11), and the servants who praise him are those who have been freed from bondage to idols (1 Thessalonians 1:9) to serve the living God.
2: You who stand in the LORD's house and serve in the courts of our God. The servants who stand in the LORD's house are ultimately the royal priesthood of all believers (1 Peter 2:5,9) who offer spiritual sacrifices through Jesus Christ; they stand in the true house of God, whose builder is Christ (Hebrews 3:3-6), and whose courts are the heavenly sanctuary.
3: Praise the LORD, for the LORD is good; sing praises to his name, for it is a delight. Jesus is the goodness of God made visible: when the rich young ruler called him good teacher, Jesus pointed beyond himself to the Father whose goodness he embodies (Mark 10:18); yet he who embodies divine goodness is the one whose name is above all names (Philippians 2:9), the delight of all who know him.
4: The LORD chose Jacob for himself, Israel as his prized possession. The election of Israel finds its ultimate purpose and goal in Christ, the true Israel who recapitulates the nation's history (Matthew 2:15 citing Hosea 11:1); in him the Gentiles become fellow heirs, chosen before the foundation of the world (Ephesians 1:4,11), the treasured possession of the new covenant (Titus 2:14; 1 Peter 2:9).
5: I know that the LORD is great; our Lord stands above every so-called god. Jesus demonstrated his greatness over every competing power: over demons (Mark 1:27), over death (John 11:43-44), over all rulers and authorities (Ephesians 1:21; Colossians 2:10,15). He is Lord over every so-called lord and God above every so-called god (1 Corinthians 8:5-6), the one through whom all things exist.
6: The LORD does whatever he pleases in heaven and on earth, in the seas and every depth. The crowds marveled that Jesus did whatever he pleased with absolute authority: he commanded winds and waves (Matthew 8:27), drove out demons (Mark 1:27), forgave sins (Mark 2:5-12), raised the dead (John 11:43) — doing in earth and sea and depth exactly what this psalm declares the LORD alone does.
7: He draws up clouds from the ends of the earth, makes lightning flash with the rain, and brings out the wind from his storehouses. The creator-God who commands the weather patterns is the same Lord who stilled the storm (Matthew 8:26); the disciples' awestruck question — who then is this, that even the wind and sea obey him? — is answered here: it is the LORD who brings out the wind from his storehouses.
8: He struck down the firstborn of Egypt, both people and animals. The tenth plague that forced the Exodus foreshadows Christ: the blood of the Passover lamb protected Israel's firstborn (Exodus 12:13), and Christ is the Passover Lamb sacrificed for us (1 Corinthians 5:7). God did not spare his own Son but delivered him up for all (Romans 8:32) — the ultimate firstborn given so that many firstborn sons might be brought to glory (Hebrews 2:10).
9: He sent signs and wonders into Egypt, striking Pharaoh and all his servants. The signs and wonders of the Exodus are the template for the signs and wonders of Jesus' ministry (Acts 2:22 — a man attested by God through signs and wonders); and Peter's words show the anti-type: just as God delivered Israel through signs and wonders, he raised Jesus through the greatest sign of all — resurrection.
10: He defeated great nations and put mighty kings to death. The defeated kings of Canaan typify the principalities and powers defeated at the cross; Paul declares that Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame (Colossians 2:15), triumphing over them in the cross event that is the new conquest narrative.
11: Sihon king of the Amorites, Og king of Bashan, and all the kingdoms of Canaan. The conquest of these giant kingdoms is a type of the cosmic victory Christ accomplished: he entered the strong man's house and bound the strong man (Matthew 12:29); Og and Sihon represent the demonic powers that Christ despoiled, leading captives in his victory procession (Ephesians 4:8).
12: He gave their land as an inheritance to his people Israel. The land inheritance is a type of the eschatological inheritance; Christ is the heir of all things (Hebrews 1:2), and in him believers become co-heirs with him (Romans 8:17; Ephesians 1:11) of an imperishable, undefiled inheritance kept in heaven (1 Peter 1:4), greater than any land of Canaan.
13: O LORD, your name endures forever; your memory is kept alive through every generation. The name of the LORD that endures forever is the name Jesus, exalted above every name (Philippians 2:9-11); his memory is kept alive through every generation in the eucharist — Do this in remembrance of me (Luke 22:19; 1 Corinthians 11:25-26) — until he comes.
14: The LORD will uphold the cause of his people and show compassion toward his servants. Christ is the advocate who upholds the cause of his people before the Father (1 John 2:1; Romans 8:34); his compassion, which moved him to heal and teach throughout his ministry (Matthew 9:36; Psalms 14:14), flows from the eternal character described here as the LORD's defining disposition toward his servants.
15: The idols of the nations are silver and gold — the work of human hands. Paul echoes this exactly: we know that an idol is nothing in the world, and there is no God but one (1 Corinthians 8:4); in Athens he declared that the God who made the world does not live in temples made with human hands (Acts 17:24-25), and the audience is this psalm's implicit crowd of idol-worshippers invited to turn from worthless things to the living God (Acts 14:15).
16: They have mouths but cannot speak; eyes but cannot see. The idol's inability to see, hear, or speak stands in stark contrast to the Word made flesh (John 1:14) who speaks divine truth (John 1:18), the Light of the world who truly sees (John 4:29; Psalms 9:39), the one who opens blind eyes (Matthew 11:5). Christ is the anti-idol — the living image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15).
17: They have ears but cannot hear; there is not a breath of air in their mouths. The breathless idols contrast with the one who breathed on his disciples and said receive the Holy Spirit (John 20:22); and with the one who, like the divine wind of Genesis 2:7, breathes eternal life into those who trust him (John 6:63; 2 Corinthians 3:6). The idol has no breath; Christ gives the Spirit that gives life.
18: Those who make them become like them — and so will all who trust in them. The sobering warning that idol-worshippers become as sightless and lifeless as the idols they worship (Psalms 115:8 parallel) is reversed by the gospel: those who fix their eyes on Christ are transformed into his likeness from one degree of glory to another (2 Corinthians 3:18); beholding the Lord changes his people into what they see.
19: Praise the LORD, O house of Israel! Praise the LORD, O house of Aaron! The summoning of Israel, Aaron, and Levi to praise foreshadows the unified doxology of the new Israel: the royal priesthood (1 Peter 2:9) drawn from every tribe, language, people, and nation (Revelation 5:9-10) who offer priestly praise through Christ to the Father.
20: Praise the LORD, O house of Levi! All who fear the LORD, praise the LORD! The circle of praise expands from Levi to all who fear the LORD — the God-fearers among the nations — exactly the trajectory of the NT mission: from Jewish Christians to proselytes to Gentiles (Acts 10:2,35; Psalms 13:16,26); all who fear him are welcome in the praise-circle of the new covenant community.
21: Praise the LORD from Zion — he who makes his home in Jerusalem. The LORD who makes his home in Zion has built his final dwelling in the new Jerusalem, the heavenly city (Hebrews 12:22; Revelation 21:2-3); and his dwelling among his people is through Christ the temple (John 2:19-21; Revelation 21:22), the one in whom the fullness of God dwells bodily (Colossians 1:19; Psalms 2:9).
